description.task: Create a custom thread class by extending Thread and overriding run(). Start three thread instances and observe their execution.
instructions: Observe that the output order of Thread-A and Thread-B steps may vary on each run (non-deterministic).
In a comment, explain why the output order is not guaranteed.
NOTE: the expected output below shows ONE possible ordering — your output may differ.
description.hint: Threads are scheduled by the OS/JVM. Always call thread.start() (not thread.run()) — start() creates a new thread; run() would just execute in the current thread.
description.expectedOutput:
Thread-A - Step 1
Thread-A - Step 2
Thread-A - Step 3
Thread-B - Step 1
Thread-B - Step 2
Thread-B - Step 3

description.task: Implement thread behaviour using the Runnable interface instead of extending Thread. Pass the Runnable to a Thread object.
description.context: Implementing Runnable is preferred over extending Thread because: (1) your class can still extend another class, and (2) it separates the task (Runnable) from the thread (Thread).
instructions: Note that join() makes the output deterministic — t2 starts only after t1 finishes.
In a comment, explain the difference between extending Thread vs implementing Runnable.
Submit the code with the comment.
description.hint: join() blocks the calling thread until the joined thread completes. This ensures ordered output in this demo.
description.expectedOutput:
Hello from Task 1
Hello from Task 1
Hello from Task 2
Hello from Task 2
Both tasks done.

description.task: Demonstrate a race condition on a shared counter, then fix it using the synchronized keyword.
description.context: When multiple threads modify shared data without synchronisation, the result is unpredictable (race condition). The synchronized keyword ensures only one thread at a time can execute the critical section.
instructions: Run the starter code as-is — note the synchronized keyword on increment().
Verify the output is always exactly 2000 (1000 increments × 2 threads).
In a comment, remove 'synchronized' from increment() temporarily, run several times, and note that the count is sometimes less than 2000.
Re-add 'synchronized' and submit the working code with your comment explaining the race condition.
description.hint: Without synchronized, two threads can read the same count value simultaneously, both increment it, and write back — resulting in only one actual increment instead of two (lost update problem).
